- **A.** archaeological excavations
- **B.** the Rig Veda  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Jatak Katha
- **D.** contemporary culture

**Explanation:** The Rig Veda is the oldest and most direct literary source for understanding the Early Vedic period (c. 1500–1200 BCE), containing hymns and descriptions of Aryan life, rituals, and society from that era. Why not the others: (A) archaeological excavations from the Early Vedic period are limited and sparse, making them insufficient as a primary knowledge source; (C) the Jataka Katha dates to much later periods and concerns Buddhist teachings, not the Early Vedic age; (D) contemporary culture is vague and does not refer to documented historical sources for the Early Vedic period.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001012)*

---

**Q845. Which day is observed as World Food Day?**

- **A.** September 10
- **B.** August 16
- **C.** November 4
- **D.** October 16  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** World Food Day is observed on October 16 each year to commemorate the founding of the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations on that date in 1945. This day raises awareness about hunger and malnutrition globally. Why not the others: (A) September 10 is not designated as World Food Day; (B) August 16 is not the date of FAO's founding or any related food observance; (C) November 4 has no connection to World Food Day or the FAO's establishment.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033003)*

---

**Q846. Tolkappiyam is associated with the**

- **A.** first Sangam period
- **B.** second Sangam period  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** third Sangam period
- **D.** post-third Sangam period

**Explanation:** Tolkappiyam, an ancient Tamil grammatical text, is traditionally associated with the second Sangam period according to Tamil literary history. This text is considered one of the oldest Tamil literary works and is attributed to the grammarian Tolkappiyar. Why not the others: (A) the first Sangam period is considered even more ancient and legendary with no surviving works; (C) the third Sangam period produced works like the Silappatikaram and Manimekhalai, which came later than Tolkappiyam; (D) post-third Sangam period refers to the era after the classical Sangam age had ended, which is too late for this foundational grammatical work.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001011)*

---

**Q847. The term 'Yavanapriya' mentioned in ancient Sanskrit texts denoted**

- **A.** ivory
- **B.** pepper  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** a fine variety of Indian muslin
- **D.** damsels sent to the Greek court for dance performance

**Explanation:** The term 'Yavanapriya' (meaning 'beloved of the Greeks' or 'favored by the Yavanas') referred to pepper, a highly valued spice in ancient trade that was in great demand among Greek and Roman merchants. Pepper was one of the most prized commodities in Indo-Greek trade, justifying its name as something especially cherished by the foreign traders. Why not the others: (A) ivory was valuable but not specifically termed Yavanapriya in Sanskrit texts; (C) while Indian muslin was indeed exported, it was not designated by this particular term; (D) there is no historical evidence that this term referred to any personnel sent to foreign courts.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001007)*

---

**Q848. Tulsidas, the author of Ramcharitmanas , was a contemporary of which of the following rulers?**

- **A.** Akbar  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Humayun
- **C.** Shahjahan
- **D.** Sher Shah Suri

**Explanation:** Tulsidas (1532–1623) lived during the reign of Akbar (1556–1605), making them contemporaries. Tulsidas composed the Ramcharitmanas in Awadhi during this period, and Akbar's reign was a time of significant cultural and literary patronage in North India. Why not the others: (B) Humayun's reign (1530–1540, 1555–1556) largely preceded Tulsidas's birth and active literary period; (C) Shahjahan ruled from 1628–1658, after Tulsidas's death; (D) Sher Shah Suri's rule (1540–1545) ended well before Tulsidas began his major literary work.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001010](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001010)*

---

## History and INM — Standard 8

**Q849. The decade 1981-1990 was observed as International Decade for**

- **A.** Peace
- **B.** Drinking Water Supply and Sanitation  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Women
- **D.** Children

**Explanation:** The decade 1981-1990 was declared by the United Nations as the International Drinking Water Supply and Sanitation Decade, reflecting a global commitment to improving access to clean water and sanitation infrastructure in developing nations. Why not the others: (A) Peace — while peace initiatives existed, the 1980s did not have a specific UN-declared decade dedicated to peace; (C) Women — the UN Decade for Women occurred in 1975-1985, which overlaps but is a different designation; (D) Children — the UN Decade of the Child was 1979-1988, a separate initiative.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033003)*

---

**Q850. Vikramaditya, a king of Ujjain, started the Vikrama samvat in 58 BC in commemoration of his victory over**

- **A.** Indo-Greeks
- **B.** Sakas  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Parthinas
- **D.** Kushanas

**Explanation:** Vikramaditya of Ujjain started the Vikrama samvat (era) in 58 BC to commemorate his victory over the Sakas, who were Central Asian invaders threatening northern India during that period. Why not the others: (A) Indo-Greeks — they were defeated and declined earlier, not the occasion for this era; (C) Parthians — not the primary adversary Vikramaditya commemorated; (D) Kushanas — they rose to prominence later and were not the focus of this particular victory commemoration.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001005)*

---

**Q851. Pulakesin II was the most famous ruler of**

- **A.** Chalukyas  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Cholas
- **C.** Pallavas
- **D.** Satavahanas

**Explanation:** Pulakesin II was the most celebrated ruler of the Chalukya dynasty, known for his military conquests, administrative reforms, and the expansion of Chalukyan power in the Deccan during the 7th century CE. He is particularly remembered for his conflicts with the Pallavas and his efforts to establish Chalukyan dominance in peninsular India. Why not the others: (B) Cholas were a major South Indian dynasty but reached their peak under Rajaraja I and Rajendra I, centuries after Pulakesin II; (C) Pallavas were Pulakesin II's contemporaries and rivals, not his dynasty; (D) Satavahanas had already declined significantly before Pulakesin II's era in the 7th century.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001011)*

---

**Q852. The Uprising of 1857 was described as the first Indian war of Independence by**

- **A.** S.N. Sen
- **B.** R.C. Mazumdar
- **C.** B.G. Tilak
- **D.** V.D. Savakar  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** V.D. Savarkar, in his work 'The Indian War of Independence 1857,' was the first historian to systematically describe the 1857 Uprising as India's first war of independence rather than merely a mutiny or rebellion, giving it nationalist significance. Why not the others: (A) S.N. Sen wrote about the 1857 revolt but did not originate the 'first war of independence' characterization; (B) R.C. Mazumdar was a historian who actually challenged the nationalist interpretation and viewed it more cautiously; (C) B.G. Tilak was a freedom fighter and nationalist but did not author the specific formulation that became widely associated with this interpretation.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001014)*

---

**Q853. 'Teacher's Day' is observed on which of the date?**

- **A.** September 5  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** January 30
- **C.** November 14
- **D.** October 2

**Explanation:** Teacher's Day in India is observed on September 5 to commemorate the birth anniversary of Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan, a renowned educationist and the second President of India who believed that teachers play a crucial role in nation-building. Why not the others: (B) January 30 marks Martyrs' Day, commemorating Mahatma Gandhi's assassination; (C) November 14 is Jawaharlal Nehru's birthday, observed as Children's Day; (D) October 2 is the birth anniversary of Mahatma Gandhi, observed as Gandhi Jayanti.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033002)*

---

**Q854. The system of competitive examination for civil service was accepted in principle in the year**

- **A.** 1833
- **B.** 1853  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** 1858
- **D.** 1882

**Explanation:** The system of competitive examination for civil service in British India was accepted in principle in 1853 following Lord Macaulay's recommendations and the Despatch on Education, which advocated merit-based selection instead of patronage for administrative positions. Why not the others: (A) 1833 predates the formal acceptance of the competitive examination principle; (C) 1858 marks post-Rebellion reforms but not the original acceptance of the competitive system; (D) 1882 is too late and refers to later administrative changes, not the initial acceptance of competitive examinations.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001002)*

---

**Q855. 'Rosh Hashanah' is the new year's day of which communities?**

- **A.** Jews  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Muslims
- **C.** Hindus
- **D.** Shinto

**Explanation:** Rosh Hashanah is the Jewish New Year, celebrated in the autumn according to the Hebrew calendar and observed with religious services, family gatherings, and traditional foods. It marks the beginning of the High Holy Days in Judaism. Why not the others: (B) Muslims celebrate Hijri New Year (Islamic New Year) on Muharram 1st, not Rosh Hashanah; (C) Hindus observe their new year primarily through festivals like Ugadi or Diwali, not Rosh Hashanah; (D) Shinto followers in Japan observe New Year traditions distinct from Jewish religious observances.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033004)*

---

**Q856. To meet the educational needs of the people, the Madarasa-I Nasiri was built in the region of**

- **A.** Qutub-ud-din Aibak
- **B.** Iltutmish  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Ruknuddin Firoz Shah
- **D.** Jalal-id-din Khilji

**Explanation:** The Madarasa-I Nasiri was built during the reign of Iltutmish (1211-1236 CE), the third ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, as part of his efforts to promote Islamic education and meet the educational needs of the people in his realm. Iltutmish was known for establishing institutions of learning and patronizing scholars. Why not the others: (A) Qutub-ud-din Aibak founded the Delhi Sultanate but is primarily known for military conquests and the construction of the Qutub Minar, not this madarasa; (C) Ruknuddin Firoz Shah had a very brief and unstable reign with little time or capacity for educational patronage; (D) Jalal-ud-din Khilji ruled much later (1290-1296 CE) and is not associated with the establishment of this particular institution.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001010](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001010)*

---

**Q857. The use of Kharoshti in ancient Indian architecture is the result of India's contact with**

- **A.** Central Asia
- **B.** Iran
- **C.** Greece  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** China

**Explanation:** Kharoshti was a script used in ancient India that developed as a result of cultural and trade contact with Greek-influenced regions, particularly following Alexander the Great's conquests and the subsequent Hellenistic period in northwestern India. This script reflects the blending of Indian and Greek linguistic traditions. Why not the others: (A) Central Asia had influence on Indian culture but was not the primary source of Kharoshti script development; (B) Iran's influence on India was more related to Achaemenid administration rather than the Kharoshti script specifically; (D) China's contact with India came later through trade routes and did not contribute to Kharoshti's origins.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001003)*

---

**Q858. The Timariots Governors and the Revenue Contractors, on their part reason in this manner: "Why should the neglected state of this land create uneasiness in our minds and why should we expend our money and time to render it fruitful? We may be deprived of it in a single moment, and our exertions would benefit neither ourselves nor our children." This statement was made by**

- **A.** Monserrate
- **B.** Tavernier
- **C.** Manrique
- **D.** Bernier  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** Francois Bernier, a French traveler and physician who visited India during Aurangzeb's reign in the late 17th century, made this observation about the lack of incentive for Timariots and revenue contractors to improve land, since they held positions at the pleasure of the ruler and could lose them instantly. This statement reflects Bernier's critical analysis of the Mughal administrative system and its economic consequences. Why not the others: (A) Monserrate was a Portuguese Jesuit who visited during Akbar's reign and focused on religious and court observations, not revenue administration; (B) Tavernier was a French jeweler-trader primarily interested in commerce and trade routes, not detailed critiques of land revenue systems; (C) Manrique was a Spanish friar whose accounts focused on religious conditions and missionary work rather than revenue contractor incentives.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001007)*

---

**Q859. The term Nirgrantha is associated with**

- **A.** Ajivikas
- **B.** Charvakas
- **C.** Jainas  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Pasupatas

**Explanation:** Nirgrantha literally means 'without knots or bonds' and refers to monks who have renounced worldly attachments; this term is specifically associated with Jainism, where Mahavira and his followers were called Nirgranthas because they practiced complete detachment from material possessions and worldly ties. Why not the others: (A) Ajivikas followed a different ascetic philosophy based on fatalism and determinism, not the concept of being 'without bonds'; (B) Charvakas were materialists who rejected asceticism and the concept of renunciation entirely; (D) Pasupatas were a Shaivite sect with different philosophical and monastic practices not characterized by the Nirgrantha designation.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001013)*

---

**Q860. World Literacy Day is observed on**

- **A.** Sep 5
- **B.** Aug 6
- **C.** Sep 8  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Oct 24

**Explanation:** World Literacy Day is observed on September 8 each year to promote literacy and highlight the importance of education worldwide. This date was chosen by UNESCO to commemorate the founding of the UNESCO Institute for Education in 1966. Why not the others: (A) September 5 is the date of Teachers' Day in India, not World Literacy Day; (B) August 6 is the date associated with the atomic bombing of Hiroshima, not World Literacy Day; (D) October 24 is the date of United Nations Day, not World Literacy Day.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033005)*

---

**Q861. The Upanishads are**

- **A.** a source of Hindu philosophy  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** books of ancient Hindu laws
- **C.** books on social behavior of man
- **D.** prayers to God

**Explanation:** The Upanishads are philosophical texts that form the foundation of Hindu metaphysical thought, exploring concepts like Brahman, Atman, and the nature of reality, making them a primary source of Hindu philosophy. Why not the others: (B) while they contain ethical guidance, they are not law codes like the Dharmaśāstras; (C) though they address human conduct, their primary focus is metaphysical and spiritual philosophy rather than social behavior; (D) they are philosophical treatises, not devotional prayer texts, though they may contain spiritual reflections.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001012)*

---

**Q862. When is the World Population Day observed?**

- **A.** May 31
- **B.** October 4
- **C.** December 10
- **D.** July 11  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** World Population Day is observed on July 11 each year, as designated by the United Nations to raise awareness about global population issues and demographic challenges. This date commemorates the day when the world population reached 5 billion people in 1987. Why not the others: (A) May 31 is World No Tobacco Day; (B) October 4 is World Animal Welfare Day; (C) December 10 is Human Rights Day.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/)*

---

**Q863. There were widespread risings against the British in the 1820s. Which one of the following did not revolt in the 1820s?**

- **A.** Santhals  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Ahoms
- **C.** Pagal Panthis
- **D.** Ramosi

**Explanation:** The Santhals did not revolt against the British in the 1820s; their major uprising, the Santhal Rebellion, occurred in 1855-56. The other three groups were indeed involved in significant revolts during the 1820s: the Ahoms revolted in 1828-29, the Pagal Panthis (led by Titumir) rebelled in 1831 (late 1820s-early 1830s period), and the Ramosi rose in revolt during the 1820s. Why not the others: (B) The Ahoms conducted a major rebellion in Assam during 1828-29; (C) The Pagal Panthis revolt occurred in the late 1820s-early 1830s in Bengal; (D) The Ramosi uprising took place in the 1820s in western India.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001006)*

---

**Q864. 'The Vedas contain all the truth was interpreted by**

- **A.** Swami Vivekananda
- **B.** Swami Dayananda  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Raja Rammohan Roy
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** Swami Dayananda Saraswati, founder of the Arya Samaj, was instrumental in interpreting the Vedas as containing all knowledge and truth, emphasizing Vedic authority and rationalism in Hindu reform. He promoted the idea that the Vedas are the ultimate source of all valid knowledge and need to be understood in their pure form. Why not the others: (A) Vivekananda focused on Vedantic philosophy and universal religion rather than the specific doctrine that Vedas contain all truth; (C) Raja Rammohan Roy emphasized reason and social reform within a theistic framework but did not champion this particular Vedic interpretation; (D) The question seeks a specific historical figure, not 'none.'}

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001009](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001009)*

---

**Q865. Under Akbar, the Mir Bakshi was required to look after**

- **A.** military affairs  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** the state treasury
- **C.** the royal household
- **D.** the land revenue system

**Explanation:** The head of the military was called the Mir Bakshi, appointed from among the leading nobles of the court. The Mir Bakshi was in charge of intelligence gathering, and also made recommendations to the emperor for military appointments and promotions.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/)*

---

**Q866. To which professions earlier leaders who struggled for freedom of India mainly belonged?**

- **A.** Lawyers
- **B.** Teachers
- **C.** Journalists
- **D.** All of the above  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The correct answer is (D) All of the above, because India's freedom struggle drew leaders from multiple professions. Prominent freedom fighters like Jawaharlal Nehru, M.A. Jinnah, and B.R. Ambedkar were lawyers; figures like Sri Aurobindo and Abul Kalam Azad had teaching backgrounds; and leaders like Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Subodh Chandra were journalists or editors. The nationalist movement was multi-professional, encompassing intellectuals and professionals from various walks of life. Why not the others: (A) restricts the answer to only one profession when multiple professions were represented; (B) similarly limits to teachers alone, ignoring lawyers and journalists who played major roles; (C) focuses only on journalists, excluding the significant contributions of lawyers and educators.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001004)*

## History and INM — Standard 9

**Q867. Two of the great Mughals wrote their own memories. There were**

- **A.** Babar and Humayun
- **B.** Humayun and Jahangir
- **C.** Babar and Jahangir  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Jahangir and Shahjahan

**Explanation:** Babar and Jahangir were the two great Mughals who wrote their own memoirs. Babar composed the Babur-nama (also called Tuzuk-i-Baburi), a detailed account of his life and campaigns, while Jahangir wrote the Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri, his personal journal covering his reign. Why not the others: (A) Humayun did not write his own memoirs, though his life was documented by others; (B) while Jahangir wrote memoirs, Humayun did not; (D) Shahjahan did not compose memoirs in the same autobiographical manner as Babar and Jahangir.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001005)*

---

**Q868. January 15 is celebrated as the**

- **A.** Republic Day
- **B.** Ugadhi
- **C.** Teachers' Day
- **D.** Army Day  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** January 15 is celebrated as Army Day in India to commemorate the day when Field Marshal K. M. Kariappa took over as the first Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army in 1949, replacing the last British Commander-in-Chief. Why not the others: (A) Republic Day is observed on January 26, not January 15; (B) Ugadhi is a Telugu New Year celebrated in March-April, not January; (C) Teachers' Day is celebrated on September 5, commemorating Dr. Radhakrishnan's birth anniversary.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/)*

---

**Q869. Tripitakas are sacred books of**

- **A.** Buddhists  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Hindus
- **C.** Jains
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The Tripitaka (literally 'three baskets') comprises the earliest and most authoritative Buddhist scriptures, consisting of the Vinaya Pitaka (monastic rules), Sutta Pitaka (teachings of Buddha), and Abhidhamma Pitaka (philosophical analysis). Why not the others: (B) Hindus follow the Vedas, Upanishads, and other texts rather than the Tripitaka; (C) Jains have their own sacred texts called Agamas; (D) the Tripitaka is definitively associated with Buddhism, making 'None of the above' incorrect.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/)*

---

**Q870. The term samanta , meaning a feudatory from the sixth century AD, originally meant a**

- **A.** slave
- **B.** cultivator
- **C.** neighbor  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** foreigner

**Explanation:** The term 'samanta' in Sanskrit originally meant 'neighbor' or 'one dwelling nearby,' derived from 'sama' (same/together) and referring to those occupying adjacent territories. Over time, from the sixth century AD onwards, it evolved to denote feudatory lords or vassals in the political hierarchy of early medieval India. Why not the others: (A) slave — samanta never referred to an enslaved person but to a person of landed status; (B) cultivator — while some samantas may have been agriculturalists, the original etymological meaning was not tied to farming; (D) foreigner — samanta had no connection to foreign origin but rather to geographic proximity.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001009](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001009)*

---

**Q871. Velu Thampi led a revolt against the British in state of**

- **A.** Travancore  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Baroda
- **C.** Hyderabad
- **D.** Mysore

**Explanation:** Velu Thampi Dalawa was the chief minister (Dalawa) of Travancore who led the famous Velu Thampi Revolt in 1809 against British colonial interference and the Raja's dependence on British forces. He is a significant figure in Kerala's anti-colonial resistance movement. Why not the others: (B) Baroda was associated with revolts by figures like Khanderao and later conflicts, not Velu Thampi; (C) Hyderabad had its own resistance movements but Velu Thampi was specifically active in Travancore; (D) Mysore's notable revolt was led by Tipu Sultan against the British, not Velu Thampi.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001006)*

**Q872. The victories of Karikala are well portrayed in**

- **A.** Palamoli
- **B.** Aruvanad
- **C.** Pattinappalai  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Padirrupattu

**Explanation:** Pattinappalai is a sangam literature text that specifically celebrates the maritime glory and military victories of Karikala, the Chola king, particularly his naval prowess and control over trade routes. This akam poem is one of the primary literary sources describing Karikala's achievements. Why not the others: (A) Palamoli is not a recognized sangam text associated with Karikala's victories; (B) Aruvanad is not a primary sangam literature source that documents Karikala's conquests; (D) Padirrupattu is a collection of poems but does not specifically focus on portraying Karikala's victories.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001004)*

**Q873. The twenty-third Jaina teacher, Parsva, the immediate predecessor of Mahavira enjoined on his disciples four great vows. To these Mahavira addes which of the followings as the fifth vow?**

- **A.** Abstention from stealing
- **B.** Non-injury
- **C.** Brahmacharya or continence  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Non-attachment

**Explanation:** Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara and founder of Jainism as we know it, retained the four great vows established by his predecessor Parsva (non-violence, truth, non-stealing, and non-attachment) and added Brahmacharya (chastity or continence) as the fifth vow to create a more rigorous ethical code for Jain monks and nuns. Why not the others: (A) Abstention from stealing was already one of Parsva's four vows, not a new addition by Mahavira; (B) Non-injury (ahimsa) was the first and foundational vow of Parsva, not introduced by Mahavira; (D) Non-attachment was similarly part of Parsva's original four vows, not a Mahavira innovation.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001014)*

---

**Q874. The Kalinga was fought in**

- **A.** 321 BC
- **B.** 301 BC
- **C.** 261 BC  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** 241 BC

**Explanation:** The Kalinga War was fought in 261 BC during the reign of Emperor Ashoka, marking a significant turning point in his life as he witnessed the devastation of warfare and subsequently converted to Buddhism and pacifism. Why not the others: (A) 321 BC was the period of Chandragupta Maurya's early reign, before Ashoka; (B) 301 BC occurred during Bindusara's reign, Ashoka's father, not Ashoka's time; (D) 241 BC was after Ashoka's death and the decline of the Mauryan Empire.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001013)*

---

**Q875. The birthday of which of the leader in India is observed as Children's day?**

- **A.** Mahatma Gandhi
- **B.** S.Radhakrishnan
- **C.** Rajiv Gandhi
- **D.** Jawaharlal Nehru  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** Jawaharlal Nehru's birthday on November 14 is observed as Children's Day in India because of his deep affection for children and his vision for their development as the future of the nation. Why not the others: (A) Mahatma Gandhi's birthday (October 2) is observed as Gandhi Jayanti, a national holiday; (B) S. Radhakrishnan's birthday (September 5) is observed as Teachers' Day to honor his contributions to education; (C) Rajiv Gandhi was a later Prime Minister and his birthday is not observed as a national children's day.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033003)*

**Q876. The term Brahmadeya occurs for the first time in**

- **A.** early Vedic texts
- **B.** early Buddhist texts
- **C.** pre-Gupta inscriptions  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** post-Gupta inscriptions

**Explanation:** Brahmadeya, referring to land grants given to Brahmins, first appears in pre-Gupta inscriptions from the early centuries of the Common Era, marking the beginning of documented evidence for this practice. Why not the others: (A) early Vedic texts predate the actual practice of formalized Brahmadeya grants as a documented institution; (B) early Buddhist texts do not contain references to Brahmadeya, which was a Hindu practice; (D) post-Gupta inscriptions show the continuation and proliferation of Brahmadeya grants, but not their first occurrence.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001011)*

**Q877. Which year is observed as Poverty Eradication Year by SAARC?**

- **A.** 1998
- **B.** 1997
- **C.** 1996
- **D.** 1995  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** SAARC designated 1995 as the Poverty Eradication Year to focus collective regional attention on addressing poverty in South Asian countries through coordinated initiatives and awareness. Why not the others: (A) 1998 was not designated as SAARC's Poverty Eradication Year; (B) 1997 was not the year chosen by SAARC for this specific observance; (C) 1996 does not correspond to SAARC's official Poverty Eradication Year declaration.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033004)*

**Q878. Vaikhanasa the five-fold conception of Vishnu consists of brahman purusha prakriti satya achyuta aniruddha**

- **A.** I, II, III, IV and V
- **B.** II, III, IV, V and VI
- **C.** I, II, IV, V and VI
- **D.** I, III, IV, V and VI  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The Vaikhanasa school's five-fold conception of Vishnu (Pancharupa) includes brahman (I), prakriti (III), satya (IV), achyuta (V), and aniruddha (VI)—these five forms represent different aspects of the divine according to Vaikhanasa philosophy. Why not the others: (A) incorrectly includes purusha (II) instead of satya or aniruddha; (B) excludes brahman (I) which is fundamental to the conception; (C) includes purusha (II) which is not part of the authentic five-fold conception.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001003)*

---

**Q879. The World Environment Day is celebrated on**

- **A.** April 7
- **B.** June 5  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** August 6
- **D.** June 16

**Explanation:** World Environment Day is celebrated on June 5 every year, marking the date of the opening of the United Nations Conference on the Human Environment in Stockholm in 1972. This date was officially designated by the United Nations to raise global awareness about environmental issues and promote action for environmental protection. Why not the others: (A) April 7 is World Health Day; (C) August 6 commemorates the atomic bombing of Hiroshima; (D) June 16 is Youth Day in South Africa and is not the international environmental day.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/days-and-years/033002)*

**Q880. Universities in the Presidency towns in India were established in**

- **A.** 1857  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** 1858
- **C.** 1900
- **D.** 1909

**Explanation:** Universities in the Presidency towns (Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras) were established in 1857 following the recommendations of the Wood's Dispatch of 1854, which sought to expand higher education in British India. These three universities became the first major universities in India. Why not the others: (B) 1858 is too late and marks no significant educational milestone; (C) 1900 is far too late and falls well after the Presidency universities were already functioning; (D) 1909 refers to a later period when other universities were being established, but not the original Presidency universities.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001012)*

---

**Q881. Through which one of the following, the king exercised his control over villages in the Vijayanagar Empire?**

- **A.** Dannayaka
- **B.** Sumanta
- **C.** Nayaka
- **D.** Mahanayakacharya  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The Mahanayakacharya was the supreme administrative officer in the Vijayanagar Empire who directly represented the king's authority and exercised control over villages and territorial administration on behalf of the monarch, making this the correct answer. Why not the others: (A) Dannayaka was a military commander or feudal lord responsible for military affairs, not village control; (B) Sumanta was an advisory official or minister in the court, not primarily responsible for direct village administration; (C) Nayaka was a subordinate military-feudal officer under the higher administrative hierarchy, not the principal instrument through which the king exercised village control.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001002)*

---

**Q882. The ultimate ownership of land during the post-Gupta period lay with**

- **A.** the cultivator
- **B.** the village community
- **C.** the king  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** the joint family

**Explanation:** During the post-Gupta period, the king held ultimate ownership of all land within his territory, though he granted revenue rights and cultivation rights to various groups. This represented the feudal system where the monarch was considered the supreme landlord. Why not the others: (A) the cultivator held only usufruct rights (the right to cultivate and benefit from the land), not ownership; (B) the village community had collective management rights but not ultimate ownership; (D) the joint family had hereditary cultivation rights but no claim to supreme ownership of the land.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001007)*

**Q883. The weekly Commonweal was founded by**

- **A.** Annie Besant  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Bipan Chandra Pal
- **C.** Bal Gangadhar Tilak
- **D.** Sarojini Naidu

**Explanation:** Annie Besant founded the weekly newspaper Commonweal in 1888, which became an important platform for nationalist thought and social reform during the Indian independence movement. Why not the others: (B) Bipan Chandra Pal founded the Bande Mataram newspaper, not Commonweal; (C) Bal Gangadhar Tilak established the Kesari and Marathi newspapers; (D) Sarojini Naidu was a poet and independence activist but did not found Commonweal.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001010](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-history/001010)*

## Indian Economy — Standard 10

**Q884. If the RBI adopts an expansionist open market operations policy, this means that it will**

- **A.** buy securities from non-government holders
- **B.** sell securities in the open market
- **C.** offer commercial banks more credit in the open market  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** openly announce to the market that it intends to expand credit

**Explanation:** An expansionist open market operations (OMO) policy by the RBI means increasing the money supply in the economy to encourage lending and economic growth, which is achieved by offering commercial banks more credit facilities in the open market. This allows banks to have greater liquidity for lending to the private sector. Why not the others: (A) buying securities would be a contractionary measure if interpreted narrowly, though OMO purchases do occur—but the specific phrasing of 'offering more credit' better captures the expansionist intent; (B) selling securities is a contractionary OMO policy that reduces money supply, the opposite of expansion; (D) merely announcing intentions without actual credit provision is not a monetary policy action and would have no real economic effect.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-economy/003013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-economy/003013)*

---

**Q885. How many Lok Sabha seats belong to Rajasthan?**

- **A.** 32
- **B.** 25  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** 30
- **D.** 17

**Explanation:** Rajasthan has 25 Lok Sabha seats, making it one of the states with significant representation in the lower house of Parliament. This number is based on the delimitation of constituencies in the state. Why not the others: (A) 32 is the number of Lok Sabha seats for Uttar Pradesh or another larger state, not Rajasthan; (C) 30 exceeds Rajasthan's actual allocation; (D) 17 is too low and does not correspond to Rajasthan's constituency count.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/006001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/006001)*

---

**Q886. The chief purpose of crop rotation is to check the loss of top soil**

- **A.** by water erosion
- **B.** by wind erosion
- **C.** by weathering
- **D.** of its mineral content  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The chief purpose of crop rotation is to check the loss of soil's mineral content because alternating different crops helps maintain soil fertility—legumes replenish nitrogen, different crops extract different nutrients, and this practice prevents nutrient depletion that would occur with monoculture. Why not the others: (A) water erosion is prevented through contour plowing and terracing rather than crop rotation; (B) wind erosion is addressed by windbreaks and shelter belts, not crop rotation; (C) weathering is a natural geological process that crop rotation cannot meaningfully prevent.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/019001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/019001)*

---

**Q887. What is the population density of Madhya Pradesh?**

- **A.** 202/sq. km
- **B.** 196/sq. km  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** 116/sq. km
- **D.** 68/sq. km

**Explanation:** Madhya Pradesh has a population density of approximately 196 persons per square kilometer, according to recent census data. This moderate density reflects the state's significant landmass combined with its substantial population. Why not the others: (A) 202/sq. km is slightly higher than the actual density; (C) 116/sq. km significantly underestimates the state's population concentration; (D) 68/sq. km is far too low and would apply to much more sparsely populated regions.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/023001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/023001)*

---

**Q888. Where is the permanent secretariat of the SAARC?**

- **A.** Kathmandu  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** New Delhi
- **C.** Islamabad
- **D.** Colombo

**Explanation:** The permanent secretariat of SAARC (South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation) is located in Kathmandu, Nepal, which was established as the official headquarters when SAARC was founded in 1985. Why not the others: (B) New Delhi is the capital of India, not the designated SAARC secretariat location; (C) Islamabad is Pakistan's capital but was never chosen as SAARC's permanent secretariat; (D) Colombo is Sri Lanka's capital and hosted early SAARC summits but is not the permanent secretariat.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/024001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/024001)*

---

**Q889. The headquarter of OAU (Organization of African Unity) are at**

- **A.** Addis Ababa, Ethiopia  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Washington DC
- **C.** Paris
- **D.** Jakarta, Indonesia

**Explanation:** The Organization of African Unity (now known as the African Union) was established with its headquarters in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, reflecting Ethiopia's historical significance as an independent African nation and its role in the Pan-African movement. Why not the others: (B) Washington DC is the capital of the United States, not an African location; (C) Paris is the capital of France in Europe, not relevant to an African organization; (D) Jakarta, Indonesia is in Southeast Asia and has no connection to the OAU's mandate or establishment.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/020001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/basic-general-knowledge/020001)*
