*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010005)*

---

**Q1106. One should not connect a number of electrical appliances to the same power socket because**

- **A.** this can damage the appliances due to overloading
- **B.** this can damage the domestic wiring due to overloading  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** this can damage the electrical meter
- **D.** the appliance will not get full voltage

**Explanation:** When multiple electrical appliances are connected to the same power socket, the total current drawn exceeds the safe capacity of the domestic wiring, causing excessive heat generation and potential damage to the wires, insulation, and circuit components—this is the primary safety concern. Why not the others: (A) while overloading may affect appliance performance, the main danger is to the wiring itself, not individual appliances; (C) the electrical meter is designed to handle rated household current and is not the primary component at risk; (D) voltage remains relatively constant in a parallel circuit, so appliances still receive adequate voltage even with multiple connections.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010014)*

---

## Physics — Standard 9

**Q1107. In which of the following industries is mica as a raw material?**

- **A.** Cement
- **B.** Glass and Pottery
- **C.** Iron and Steel
- **D.** Electrical  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** Mica is a silicate mineral with excellent electrical insulating properties and high heat resistance, making it the raw material of choice for the electrical industry, where it is used in capacitors, insulators, and other electrical equipment. Why not the others: (A) Cement uses limestone and clay as primary raw materials, not mica; (B) Glass and pottery use silica sand, feldspar, and clay, but mica is not a standard raw material for these; (C) Iron and steel production relies on iron ore, coal, and limestone, not mica.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010013)*

---

**Q1108. Siphon will fail to work if**

- **A.** the densities of the liquid in the two vessels are equal
- **B.** the level of the liquid in the two vessels are at the same height  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** both its limbs are of unequal length
- **D.** the temperature of the liquids in the two vessels are the same

**Explanation:** A siphon works by creating a pressure difference between two liquid levels, allowing the liquid to flow from the higher vessel to the lower one. When both vessels have liquid at the same height, there is no pressure difference to drive the flow, so the siphon cannot function. Why not the others: (A) equal densities do not prevent siphon operation, as the siphon relies on height difference rather than density difference; (C) unequal limb lengths are actually typical and necessary for siphons to work effectively; (D) temperature differences between the two vessels do not affect the basic pressure-driven mechanism of a siphon.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/)*

---

**Q1109. Moment of inertia is**

- **A.** vector
- **B.** scalar
- **C.** phasor
- **D.** tensor  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** Moment of inertia is a tensor because it is represented as a 3×3 matrix (the inertia tensor) that relates angular momentum to angular velocity, and its components depend on the choice of coordinate system and axis of rotation. Why not the others: (A) it is not a simple vector, as it cannot be represented by magnitude and direction alone; (B) while its magnitude at a given axis is a scalar, the full moment of inertia property across all axes requires tensor representation; (C) a phasor is used in AC circuit analysis and wave phenomena, not in rotational dynamics.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010007)*

---

**Q1110. Light from the Sun reaches us in nearly**

- **A.** 2 minutes
- **B.** 4 minutes
- **C.** 8 minutes  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** 16 minutes

**Explanation:** Light travels at approximately 3 × 10⁸ m/s, and the Sun is about 150 million kilometers (1 AU) away from Earth. Dividing this distance by the speed of light gives roughly 8 minutes and 20 seconds for sunlight to reach us. Why not the others: (A) 2 minutes is far too short and would suggest the Sun is only about 37.5 million km away; (B) 4 minutes represents half the correct travel time and underestimates the vast distance between Earth and Sun; (D) 16 minutes would imply the Sun is twice as far away as it actually is.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010002)*

---

**Q1111. One fathom is equal to**

- **A.** 6 feet  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** 6 meters
- **C.** 60 feet
- **D.** 100 cm

**Explanation:** A fathom is a traditional unit of length used primarily in nautical contexts to measure water depth, and it is defined as exactly 6 feet (approximately 1.83 meters). This is the standard maritime definition recognized internationally. Why not the others: (B) 6 meters is too large—that would be roughly 20 feet, not a fathom; (C) 60 feet is ten times larger than the actual fathom measurement; (D) 100 cm (1 meter) is less than half a fathom's actual length.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/038001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/038001)*

---

**Q1112. Sound of frequency below 20 Hz is called**

- **A.** audio sounds
- **B.** infrasonic  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** ultrasonic
- **D.** supersonics

**Explanation:** Sound with frequency below 20 Hz is called infrasonic because the prefix 'infra-' means below, and 20 Hz is the lower threshold of human hearing; therefore, sounds below this frequency are below the audible range. Why not the others: (A) audio sounds refer to sounds within the human hearing range (20 Hz to 20,000 Hz), not below it; (C) ultrasonic refers to sounds above 20,000 Hz, not below 20 Hz; (D) supersonics relates to objects moving faster than the speed of sound, not to sound frequency classification.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010006)*

---

**Q1113. Light year is a measurement of**

- **A.** speed of aeroplanes
- **B.** speed of light
- **C.** Stellar distances  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** speed of rockets

**Explanation:** A light year is the distance that light travels through a vacuum in one year, making it a unit of distance rather than speed. It is used to measure vast distances between celestial objects and stars, which is why it's called a stellar distance measurement. Why not the others: (A) speed of aeroplanes is measured in km/h or m/s, not light years; (B) while light year is related to the speed of light, it measures distance not speed; (D) speed of rockets is also measured in velocity units like km/h, not distance units like light years.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/038002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/038002)*

---

**Q1114. Which of the following is a protein?**

- **A.** Natural rubber  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Starch
- **C.** Cellulose
- **D.** None of these

**Explanation:** Natural rubber is a protein composed of amino acids linked by peptide bonds, making it a biological macromolecule with protein characteristics. Why not the others: (B) Starch is a carbohydrate polymer of glucose units, not a protein; (C) Cellulose is also a carbohydrate polymer of glucose units, not a protein; (D) This is incorrect because option (A) is the correct answer.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/040002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/040002)*

---

**Q1115. It is easier to roll a barrel full of coal tar than to pull it because**

- **A.** the full weight of the barrel comes into play when it is pulled
- **B.** the rolling friction is much less than the sliding friction  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** more surface area of the barrel is in contact with the road when it is pulled
- **D.** coal tar is a liquid and it flows in the barrel when it is rolled

**Explanation:** Rolling friction is substantially smaller than sliding (kinetic) friction because rolling objects have a much smaller contact area and lower energy dissipation compared to surfaces being dragged. This fundamental difference in friction types makes rolling a barrel easier than pulling it across the ground. Why not the others: (A) the weight acts equally whether rolling or pulling, so it doesn't explain why rolling is easier; (C) contact area is actually smaller when rolling than when dragging, and this difference favors rolling, not pulling; (D) coal tar's fluid nature is irrelevant to the comparison between rolling and sliding friction forces.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010015](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010015)*

---

**Q1116. Rainbow is produced when sunlight fall on drops of rain. Which of the following physical phenomena are responsible for this? Diffusion Refraction Internal reflection**

- **A.** I, II and III
- **B.** I and II
- **C.** II and III  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** I and III

**Explanation:** Rainbow formation requires refraction (bending of light as it enters and exits water droplets) and internal reflection (light bouncing inside the droplet to separate colors), both of which are essential to the rainbow phenomenon. Diffusion is not involved in rainbow formation—the process depends on the precise geometric paths of light through individual droplets, not on scattering of light in random directions. Why not the others: (A) includes diffusion, which plays no role in rainbows; (B) omits internal reflection, which is crucial for the light to bounce within droplets and reach the observer's eye; (D) includes diffusion instead of refraction, but diffusion is not a rainbow mechanism.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010014)*

---

**Q1117. An aeroplane is flying horizontally with a velocity of 600 km/h and at a height of 1960 m. When it is vertically at a point A on the ground a bomb is released from it. The bomb strikes the ground at point B. The distance AB is**

- **A.** 1200 m
- **B.** 0.33 km
- **C.** 3.33 km  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** 33 km

**Explanation:** The bomb falls vertically due to gravity while maintaining the airplane's horizontal velocity of 600 km/h. First, find the time to fall from height h = 1960 m using h = ½gt²: 1960 = ½(10)(t²), so t = 19.8 ≈ 20 seconds. The horizontal distance traveled is AB = velocity × time = 600 km/h × 20 s = (600 × 1000/3600) m/s × 20 s = 166.67 × 20 = 3333 m ≈ 3.33 km. Why not the others: (A) 1200 m is too small, resulting from an incorrect calculation of either time or velocity conversion; (B) 0.33 km is 10 times smaller than the correct answer, likely from a major calculation error in time or distance conversion; (D) 33 km is 10 times larger, possibly from doubling the time or miscalculating the velocity conversion factor.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010016](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010016)*

---

**Q1118. Which one of the following is the petroleum wax?**

- **A.** Paraffin wax  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Jonoba wax
- **C.** Carnauba wax
- **D.** Bees wax

**Explanation:** Paraffin wax is obtained from petroleum by dewaxing light lubricating oil stocks. It is used in candles, wax paper, polishes, cosmetics, and electrical insulators. It assists in extracting perfumes from flowers, forms a base for medical ointments, and supplies a waterproof coating for wood. In wood and paper matches, it helps to ignite the matchstick by supplying an easily vaporized hydrocarbon fuel.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/040005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/040005)*

---

**Q1119. Which of the following is used in pencils?**

- **A.** Graphite  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Silicon
- **C.** Charcoal
- **D.** Phosphorous

**Explanation:** Graphite is the primary material used in pencil cores because it is a form of carbon that leaves visible marks on paper while being soft enough to write smoothly without excessive pressure. Why not the others: (B) Silicon is a semiconductor element not used for writing in pencils; (C) Charcoal is a carbon form but too fragile and produces inconsistent marks compared to graphite; (D) Phosphorous is a reactive nonmetal element not suitable for pencil cores.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/)*

---

**Q1120. Lux is the SI unit of**

- **A.** intensity of illumination  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** luminous efficiency
- **C.** luminous flux
- **D.** luminous intensity

**Explanation:** The SI unit of intensity of illumination (illuminance) is the lux. An illuminance of 1.0 lux is produced by 1.0 lumen of light shining on an area of 1.0 m^2.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010005)*

---

**Q1121. Which of the following shows the masses of the three elementary particles in decreasing order?**

- **A.** Leptons, Baryons, Mesons
- **B.** Mesons, Baryons, Leptons
- **C.** Baryons, Mesons, Leptons  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Leptons, Mesons Baryons

**Explanation:** Option (C) correctly arranges elementary particles by mass in decreasing order: Baryons (such as protons and neutrons) are the heaviest, Mesons (composed of quark-antiquark pairs) are intermediate in mass, and Leptons (such as electrons and neutrinos) are the lightest. Why not the others: (A) incorrectly places the lightest particles (leptons) first; (B) reverses the order of baryons and mesons, with mesons listed as heavier; (D) incorrectly groups mesons and baryons together at the end, contradicting their actual mass hierarchy.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/039002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/039002)*

---

**Q1122. Epoxy resins are used as**

- **A.** detergents
- **B.** insecticides
- **C.** adhesives  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** moth repellents

**Explanation:** Epoxy resins are thermosetting polymers that form strong, durable bonds between surfaces and are widely used as adhesives in industrial and household applications. Why not the others: (A) detergents are surfactant-based cleaning agents with different chemical properties; (B) insecticides are pesticides designed to kill insects, not bind materials; (D) moth repellents are substances that deter insects, not function as bonding agents.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/040001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/040001)*

---

**Q1123. Supersonic plane fly with the speed**

- **A.** less than the speed of sound
- **B.** of sound
- **C.** greater than the speed of sound  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** of light

**Explanation:** A supersonic plane, by definition, flies faster than the speed of sound in air (approximately 343 m/s at sea level). The term 'supersonic' literally means 'beyond sonic' or faster than sound. Why not the others: (A) would describe subsonic flight, which is slower than sound; (B) would describe transonic or sonic flight, moving at sound speed, not faster; (D) is impossible for conventional aircraft, as the speed of light is vastly greater than any achievable mechanical speed for planes.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010011)*

---

**Q1124. Out of the following pairs, choose the pair in which the physical quantities do not have identical dimension?**

- **A.** Pressure and Young's modules
- **B.** Planck's constant and Angular momentum
- **C.** Impulse and moment of force  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Force and rate of change of linear momentum

**Explanation:** Impulse (force × time) has dimensions of [MLT⁻¹], while moment of force or torque (force × distance) has dimensions of [ML²T⁻²], making them dimensionally different. Why not the others: (A) both pressure and Young's modulus have dimensions of [ML⁻¹T⁻²]; (B) both Planck's constant and angular momentum have dimensions of [ML²T⁻¹]; (D) force and rate of change of linear momentum are identical by Newton's second law, both with dimensions [MLT⁻²].

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010003)*

---

**Q1125. In an atomic explosion, enormous energy is released which is due to**

- **A.** conversion of chemical energy into heat energy
- **B.** conversion of mechanical energy into nuclear energy
- **C.** conversion of mass into energy  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** conversion of neutrons into protons

**Explanation:** In an atomic explosion, enormous energy is released because a small amount of mass is converted directly into energy according to Einstein's mass-energy equivalence equation E=mc². Since the speed of light (c) is extremely large, even a tiny amount of mass converts to a tremendous amount of energy. Why not the others: (A) chemical reactions involve electron rearrangement and release far less energy than nuclear reactions; (B) mechanical energy alone cannot produce the magnitude of energy observed in atomic explosions; (D) neutrons do not convert into protons during fission—rather, nuclei split and release neutrons.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/039001](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/039001)*

---

**Q1126. Quartz crystals normally used in quartz clocks etc. is chemically**

- **A.** silicon dioxide  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** germanium oxide
- **C.** a mixture of germanium oxide and silicon dioxide
- **D.** sodium silicate

**Explanation:** Quartz is silicon dioxide (SiO₂), a crystalline form of silica that is widely used in quartz clocks and watches because of its piezoelectric properties, which allow it to vibrate at a stable and precise frequency when subjected to an electric field. Why not the others: (B) germanium oxide is not used in quartz clocks—germanium compounds have different properties unsuitable for timekeeping applications; (C) quartz is a pure compound, not a mixture of two oxides; (D) sodium silicate is a water-soluble compound used in different applications, not in quartz clock mechanisms.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/036002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/036002)*

---

**Q1127. ML 2 T -2 is the dimensional formula for**

- **A.** moment of inertia
- **B.** pressure
- **C.** elasticity
- **D.** couple acting on a body  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** A couple (or torque) acting on a body is defined as force multiplied by perpendicular distance, which gives dimensions of [M L² T⁻²]. Moment of inertia has dimensions [M L²] without the T⁻² component; pressure is [M L⁻¹ T⁻²], which lacks the extra L factor; elasticity (Young's modulus) is [M L⁻¹ T⁻²], also dimensionally different. Why not the others: (A) moment of inertia is [M L²], missing the T⁻² term; (B) pressure is [M L⁻¹ T⁻²], which has L⁻¹ instead of L²; (C) elasticity is [M L⁻¹ T⁻²], also lacking the correct power of L.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010012)*

---

**Q1128. Which of the following is used as a lubricant?**

- **A.** Graphite  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Silica
- **C.** Iron Oxide
- **D.** Diamond

**Explanation:** Graphite is used as a lubricant because its layered crystalline structure allows the layers to slide easily over each other, reducing friction between surfaces. Why not the others: (B) Silica is a hard, brittle material used in glass and abrasives, not as a lubricant; (C) Iron Oxide is a compound used in pigments and magnetic materials, not for reducing friction; (D) Diamond is an extremely hard abrasive material used for cutting and grinding, not for lubrication.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/036003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/036003)*

---

**Q1129. Nautical mile is a unit of distance used in**

- **A.** navigation  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** road mile
- **C.** astronomy
- **D.** measuring the boundaries of a nation

**Explanation:** A nautical mile is a unit of distance primarily used in navigation, particularly for measuring distances at sea and in aviation, where it relates directly to latitude and longitude coordinates on Earth. Why not the others: (B) road mile refers to the standard terrestrial mile used for land distances, not the nautical mile; (C) astronomy uses units like light-years and parsecs for cosmic distances, not nautical miles; (D) national boundaries are measured using standard miles or kilometers, not nautical miles.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/038004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/general-science/038004)*

---

**Q1130. Sound waves in air are**

- **A.** transverse
- **B.** longitudinal  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** electromagnetic
- **D.** polarised

**Explanation:** Sound waves in air are longitudinal waves, meaning the particles of the medium (air molecules) oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation, creating compressions and rarefactions. This mechanism of energy transfer through a medium is fundamental to how sound travels. Why not the others: (A) transverse waves have particle motion perpendicular to wave direction, which cannot propagate through air without a medium structure; (C) electromagnetic waves do not require a medium and travel via electric and magnetic fields, not particle vibrations; (D) polarization is a property of transverse waves only, and sound lacks this property.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/physics/010004)*

---

## Polity — Standard 10

**Q1131. The president can expend out of the Contingency Fund of India**

- **A.** only with the approval of the Parliament
- **B.** without the approval of the Parliament  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** only in the case of the national calamities
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The Contingency Fund of India, established under Article 267(1) of the Constitution, allows the President to make emergency expenditures without prior parliamentary approval, which is its primary purpose—to enable quick financial response in urgent situations. Why not the others: (A) contradicts the constitutional design of the fund, which specifically permits expenditure without prior parliamentary approval; (C) is too restrictive, as the fund can be used for any unforeseen emergency, not just natural calamities; (D) is incorrect because option (B) correctly states the constitutional provision.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004)*

---

**Q1132. The members of the Rajya Sabha are elected for a term**

- **A.** of six years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** determined by the state legislative assembly of a state
- **C.** of four years
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** Members of the Rajya Sabha are elected for a fixed term of six years, as specified in Article 83 of the Indian Constitution. One-third of Rajya Sabha members retire every two years, ensuring continuity and stability in the upper house. Why not the others: (B) the term is fixed by the Constitution, not determined individually by state legislatures; (C) four years is the term for Lok Sabha members, not Rajya Sabha; (D) incorrect because option A is the correct constitutional provision.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005)*

---

**Q1133. The objective of the Morley-Minto Reforms was**

- **A.** extension of provincial assemblies  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** to give more powers to local government
- **C.** to abolish the post of secretary of the state for India
- **D.** to establish diarchy in provinces

**Explanation:** The Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909 primarily aimed to expand the scope and representation of provincial legislative assemblies by increasing both their size and the number of elected representatives, thereby introducing a measure of representative governance into the colonial administration. Why not the others: (B) while the reforms touched on local bodies, their main focus was on provincial-level assemblies rather than grass-root local government; (C) the reforms did not address abolishing the Secretary of State for India, which remained a key position in British India's governance structure; (D) diarchy (division of powers between elected and appointed officials) was introduced through the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms of 1919, not the Morley-Minto Reforms.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014)*

---

**Q1134. The Nagaland State of India cannot be termed as a state because it lacks**

- **A.** sovereignty  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** bicameral legislature
- **C.** a high court exclusively of its own
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** Nagaland cannot be termed a fully sovereign state because, as a state within the Indian Union, it lacks complete sovereignty—sovereignty is vested in the Union of India, not in individual states. States in India exercise delegated powers within the constitutional framework and cannot independently exercise foreign policy, currency, or defense powers. Why not the others: (B) bicameral legislature is not essential for statehood, as many Indian states have unicameral legislatures; (C) Nagaland does not have an exclusive high court of its own but falls under the Gauhati High Court, yet this is an administrative arrangement and does not disqualify it from being a state; (D) is incorrect because the lack of sovereignty is a valid reason for such a distinction.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012)*

---

**Q1135. The minimum age of the voter in India is**

- **A.** 15 years
- **B.** 18 years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** 21 years
- **D.** 25 years

**Explanation:** The minimum age of a voter in India is 18 years, as stipulated by Article 326 of the Indian Constitution. This age requirement was established to ensure that citizens have adequate maturity and understanding to participate in democratic elections. Why not the others: (A) 15 years is below the constitutionally mandated voting age; (C) 21 years was the voting age in earlier years but was reduced to 18 in 1989; (D) 25 years is significantly higher than the actual constitutional requirement.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003)*

---

**Q1136. The Parliament enjoys legislative power over subjects in**

- **A.** the union list only
- **B.** the concurrent list only
- **C.** both union and concurrent list  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** all the three lists, viz. union list, state list and the concurrent list

**Explanation:** The Parliament of India has exclusive legislative power over subjects enumerated in the Union List and concurrent power (shared with state legislatures) over subjects in the Concurrent List. The Seventh Schedule of the Constitution divides legislative powers into these three categories, with Parliament having sole authority over Union List matters and shared authority over Concurrent List matters, while States have exclusive power over the State List. Why not the others: (A) incomplete as Parliament also legislates on concurrent subjects; (B) incomplete as Parliament also legislates on union subjects; (D) incorrect because Parliament cannot legislate on State List matters, which are reserved for state governments.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007)*

---

**Q1137. The number of writs that can be prayed for and issued by the Supreme Court and/or a High Court is**

- **A.** 3
- **B.** 4
- **C.** 5  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** 6

**Explanation:** The Supreme Court and High Courts have the power to issue five types of writs under Articles 32 and 226 of the Indian Constitution: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, and Quo Warranto. These writs are fundamental remedies for enforcing fundamental rights and ensuring proper exercise of judicial authority. Why not the others: (A) only three writs would exclude important remedies like Quo Warranto and Prohibition; (B) four writs is incomplete and misses one of the recognized categories; (D) six writs exceeds the constitutionally established types and includes non-existent writs.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002)*

---

**Q1138. The phrase 'procedure established by the law' means**

- **A.** the judges in India cannot question the fairness or validity of a law, provided it is within the limits of the constitution  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** judges in India can question the fairness or validity of an undue law even if it is within the limits of the constitution
- **C.** judges in India can declare a law invalid simply because in their opinion the law is not due or is unjust
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The phrase 'procedure established by the law' (Article 21, Indian Constitution) means that as long as a law follows the constitutional procedure and remains within constitutional limits, courts cannot strike it down merely on grounds of fairness or substantive justice—they can only review its constitutional validity. This contrasts with the American concept of 'due process,' which allows courts broader scrutiny of law's fairness. Why not the others: (B) contradicts the basic principle that judges cannot question fairness if the law is constitutionally valid; (C) overstates judicial power by suggesting judges can invalidate laws based on personal opinion about justice rather than constitutional breach; (D) is incorrect because option (A) correctly defines the established legal principle.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011)*

---

**Q1139. The members of the parliamentary committee**

- **A.** are appointed by the president in consultation with the prime minister
- **B.** are appointed by the president on the recommendation of the speaker
- **C.** are taken from various groups and parties in Parliament in proportion to their respective strength  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** both (b) and (c)

**Explanation:** Parliamentary committees in India are constituted by taking members from various groups and parties represented in Parliament in proportion to their respective strength, ensuring proportional representation of all political parties and independent members. This principle applies across most parliamentary committees to maintain fairness and allow all sections of Parliament to participate in legislative oversight. Why not the others: (A) while the President's role exists in some formal capacities, committee members are not appointed through President-PM consultation; (B) the Speaker's recommendation alone is not the primary mechanism—proportional representation from the House is the standard practice; (D) since (B) is incorrect, the combined option cannot be correct.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006)*

---

**Q1140. The new committee system constitutes an improvement over the earlier committee system in so far as**

- **A.** it assures representation to all the political parties in proportion to their strength in the Parliament
- **B.** it enables the Parliament to examine the grants of all the ministries and departments in detail  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** it enables the Parliament to accept the demands of various ministries without scrutiny
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The new committee system (introduced through various parliamentary reforms) improved legislative oversight by establishing standing committees that systematically examine the budget allocations and grant requests of all ministries and departments, enabling detailed parliamentary scrutiny rather than superficial approval. This represents a substantive improvement in Parliament's budgetary control and accountability mechanisms. Why not the others: (A) the committee system does not guarantee proportional representation of all political parties in every committee; (C) this contradicts the entire purpose of the committee system, which is to ensure scrutiny, not bypass it; (D) option (B) correctly identifies the key improvement.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015)*

---

**Q1141. The Muslim League as a political party was founded in**

- **A.** 1906  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** 1909
- **C.** 1915
- **D.** 1919

**Explanation:** The Muslim League was founded in 1906 at Dacca (now Dhaka) as a political organization to represent the interests of Muslims in British India. Why not the others: (B) 1909 marks when the Muslim League participated in the Simla Deputation but was not its founding year; (C) 1915 is when Muhammad Ali Jinnah joined the party, not when it was established; (D) 1919 is well after the party's formation and represents a different period in Indian political history.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013)*

---

**Q1142. The power to decide an election petition is vested in the**

- **A.** Parliament
- **B.** Supreme Court
- **C.** High courts  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** Election Commission

**Explanation:** Under the Representation of the People Act, 1951, the power to decide election petitions is vested in the High Courts. An election petition is a legal challenge to the validity of an election or the election of a particular candidate, and the High Court of the respective state has jurisdiction to hear and decide such petitions. Why not the others: (A) Parliament lacks the judicial authority to adjudicate election disputes as it is a legislative body; (B) the Supreme Court is not the first instance court for election petitions, though it can hear appeals against High Court decisions; (D) the Election Commission is an administrative body responsible for conducting elections, not for deciding disputes through judicial proceedings.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/)*

---

## Polity — Standard 6

**Q1143. The preamble to our constitution provided that India is**

- **A.** a sovereign, socialist and democratic republic
- **B.** a sovereign, socialist, secular and democratic republic  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** a sovereign republic with a socialist pattern of society
- **D.** a socialist, secular and democratic republic

**Explanation:** Option B is correct because the Preamble to the Indian Constitution, as amended by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1976, explicitly describes India as a 'sovereign, socialist, secular and democratic republic.' The words 'socialist' and 'secular' were inserted into the Preamble during this amendment. Why not the others: (A) omits the word 'secular,' which is a key descriptor in the current Preamble; (C) uses the phrase 'socialist pattern of society' instead of 'socialist' directly, which is imprecise; (D) omits 'sovereign,' which is a fundamental characteristic of India's constitutional framework.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011)*

---

**Q1144. The members of the state legislature exercise control over the council of the ministers through**

- **A.** questions and supplementary questions
- **B.** criticism of its policies
- **C.** adjournment motion
- **D.** All of the above  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The correct answer is (D) because members of the state legislature have multiple mechanisms to exercise control over the council of ministers, including posing questions and supplementary questions to ministers, criticizing government policies during debates, and moving adjournment motions to discuss matters of urgent public importance. Why not the others: (A) covers only one mechanism and ignores other important control methods; (B) addresses only policy criticism and misses the procedural tools like questions and adjournment motions; (C) refers to just one specific procedure and excludes questions and broader criticism as control mechanisms.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013)*

---

**Q1145. The president can assign any of the functions of the union government to the state government**

- **A.** in consultation with the chief justice of India
- **B.** in consultation with the state governor
- **C.** in his discretion
- **D.** in consultation with the government of the state  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The President cannot unilaterally reassign functions of the union government to states; this requires consultation with the state government itself, as the states are autonomous entities with their own constitutional jurisdiction. The President must obtain the consent and agreement of the state government before delegating any union functions to it. Why not the others: (A) the Chief Justice has no constitutional role in such executive delegations; (B) the Governor represents the President's authority in states and is not the appropriate consultative body for such matters; (C) the President's discretion is limited by constitutional requirements to consult the affected state government.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002016](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002016)*

---

**Q1146. The name of a candidate for the office of president of India may be proposed by**

- **A.** any five citizens of India
- **B.** any five members of the Parliament
- **C.** 50 members of electoral college and supported by another 50 members.  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** ten members of the Electoral College

**Explanation:** According to Article 57 of the Indian Constitution, a candidate for the office of President must be nominated by at least 50 members of the Electoral College (which comprises members of both houses of Parliament and State Legislative Assemblies), and the nomination must be supported by another 50 members. This ensures that only candidates with substantial support from elected representatives can contest for the presidency. Why not the others: (A) mere citizens cannot propose presidential candidates without being part of the Electoral College; (B) Parliament members alone cannot propose without the required Electoral College nomination procedure; (D) only 10 members is insufficient, as the constitutional minimum is 50 proposers and 50 seconders.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007)*

---

**Q1147. The national anthem was written by**

- **A.** Bankim Chandra Chatterjee
- **B.** Rabindranath Tagore  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Sarat Chandra Chatterjee
- **D.** Aurobindo Ghosh

**Explanation:** Rabindranath Tagore composed 'Jana Gana Mana,' which was adopted as India's national anthem in 1950. Tagore wrote this Bengali poem in 1911, and it was later translated into Hindi as the official national anthem of the Indian Republic. Why not the others: (A) Bankim Chandra Chatterjee wrote 'Vande Mataram,' which became the national song but not the national anthem; (C) Sarat Chandra Chatterjee was a novelist and did not compose the national anthem; (D) Aurobindo Ghosh was a freedom fighter and philosopher but did not write the national anthem.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012)*

---

**Q1148. The present Lok Sabha is the**

- **A.** 14th Lok Sabha
- **B.** 15th Lok Sabha
- **C.** 16th Lok Sabha
- **D.** 17th Lok Sabha  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The 17th Lok Sabha is the current one, constituted after the 2019 general elections held in India. This represents the 17th incarnation of the lower house of Parliament since India's independence and the adoption of its Constitution. Why not the others: (A) the 14th Lok Sabha was constituted in 2004 and is long past; (B) the 15th Lok Sabha was constituted in 2009 and is outdated; (C) the 16th Lok Sabha was constituted in 2014 and preceded the current one.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002)*

---

**Q1149. The pre-requisite for the enforcement of directive principles of the state policy is**

- **A.** an effective, hones government
- **B.** socialist government
- **C.** active opposition
- **D.** adequate resources  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV of the Indian Constitution) are socio-economic guidelines that the state aims to achieve for the welfare of citizens. Their enforcement requires adequate resources—financial, infrastructural, and human—because implementing policies related to education, healthcare, employment, and social security demands substantial investment and capacity. Why not the others: (A) an effective, honest government is desirable but not a strict pre-requisite; many well-intentioned governments fail due to lack of funds; (B) a socialist government is not necessary—directive principles can be pursued under any political system including democracy; (C) active opposition would hinder rather than enable enforcement of these principles.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004)*

---

**Q1150. The members of the State Public Service Commission are appointed by the**

- **A.** chief minister
- **B.** chief justice
- **C.** governor  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** vice-president

**Explanation:** The Governor of a State appoints the members of the State Public Service Commission (SPSC), as established under Article 315 of the Indian Constitution. This is part of the Governor's constitutional authority regarding state administration and institutional appointments. Why not the others: (A) the Chief Minister has no direct appointment power over SPSC members; (B) the Chief Justice has no constitutional role in SPSC appointments; (D) the Vice-President has no authority over state-level commissions.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015)*

---

**Q1151. The oath of office is conducted to the president by**

- **A.** the speaker of Lok Sabha
- **B.** the chief justice of India  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** the vice-president of India
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The President of India takes the oath of office administered by the Chief Justice of India, as prescribed in Article 60 of the Indian Constitution. This is a constitutional requirement that ensures the proper solemnization of the presidential office. Why not the others: (A) the Speaker of Lok Sabha has no constitutional role in administering the presidential oath; (C) the Vice-President administers oaths to other officials but not to the President; (D) is incorrect because there is a specific constitutional provision designating the Chief Justice for this duty.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014)*

---

**Q1152. The Parliament of India cannot be regarded as a sovereign body because**

- **A.** it can legislate only on subjects entrusted to the Centre by the Constitution
- **B.** it has to operate within the limits prescribed by the Constitution
- **C.** the Supreme Court can declare laws passed by parliament as unconstitutional if they contravene the provisions of the Constitution
- **D.** All of the above  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The correct answer is (D) because Parliament's sovereignty is limited by the Constitution in multiple ways: it can only legislate within the powers granted to the Centre (option A), it must operate within constitutional bounds (option B), and the Supreme Court can strike down laws that violate the Constitution through judicial review (option C). All three factors collectively demonstrate that Parliament is not a sovereign body but rather operates under a constitutional framework. Why not the others: (A) is only a partial explanation and misses the other crucial limitations; (B) alone does not capture the specific power distribution and judicial oversight aspects; (C) alone focuses only on judicial review without addressing structural limitations on legislative competence.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/)*

---

**Q1153. The members of a State Commission can be removed by the**

- **A.** governor on a report by the Supreme Court
- **B.** governor on a resolution passed by Parliament
- **C.** president on a report by the Supreme Court  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** president on a resolution passed by Parliament

**Explanation:** Members of a State Commission (such as the State Public Service Commission) can be removed only by the President on a report/recommendation by the Supreme Court, following the constitutional procedure outlined in Articles 317 and similar provisions. This ensures judicial oversight and maintains the independence of these constitutional bodies. Why not the others: (A) the Governor lacks the authority to remove commission members—only the President can do so; (B) Parliament cannot pass resolutions for removal of State Commission members, as this is a constitutional matter requiring Supreme Court involvement; (D) while the President does have removal authority, it must be based on a Supreme Court report, not a parliamentary resolution.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003)*

---

**Q1154. The office of the president can fall vacant due to**

- **A.** resignation
- **B.** death
- **C.** removal
- **D.** All of the above  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The office can become vacant through multiple circumstances: resignation (when the President voluntarily relinquishes office), death (natural or otherwise), and removal (through impeachment under Article 61 of the Constitution). Therefore, all three are valid reasons for presidential vacancy. Why not the others: (A) incomplete, as it covers only one mode of vacancy; (B) incomplete, as it covers only one mode of vacancy; (C) incomplete, as it covers only one mode of vacancy.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005)*

---

## Polity — Standard 7

**Q1155. The president can dismiss a member of the council of ministers**

- **A.** on his own
- **B.** on the recommendation of the prime ministers  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** only under emergency conditions
- **D.** with the consent of the speaker

**Explanation:** The President, as the constitutional head of state, cannot unilaterally dismiss a member of the Council of Ministers; instead, the President acts on the advice and recommendation of the Prime Minister, who heads the council and is responsible for its composition. This principle ensures that the executive head of government (Prime Minister) retains control over ministerial appointments and removals, maintaining accountability within the cabinet system. Why not the others: (A) the President has no independent power to dismiss ministers without PM's recommendation; (C) dismissals can occur at any time based on PM's recommendation, not only during emergencies; (D) the Speaker of the Lok Sabha has no role in dismissing ministers, as this is an executive matter.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006)*

---

**Q1156. The member of a State Public Service Commission can be removed on the ground of misbehavior only after an enquiry has been conducted by the**

- **A.** Supreme Court of India  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** High Court of the state
- **C.** committee appointed by the president
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** According to Article 317 of the Indian Constitution, a member of a State Public Service Commission can be removed on the ground of misbehavior or incapacity only after an enquiry conducted by the Supreme Court of India, ensuring a high constitutional standard for removal. Why not the others: (B) the High Court lacks the constitutional authority to conduct such enquiries for SPSC member removal; (C) the President cannot appoint a committee for this purpose as the Constitution specifically vests this power in the Supreme Court; (D) is incorrect because there is a definite constitutional procedure involving the Supreme Court.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012)*

---

**Q1157. The members of the Rajya Sabha are**

- **A.** directly elected by the people on the basis of universal adult franchise
- **B.** elected by the members of the state legislative assemblies  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** elected by the members of the state legislative councils
- **D.** elected by the members of the state legislative councils and state legislative assemblies

**Explanation:** Members of Rajya Sabha are elected by the elected members of the Assemblies of States and Union territories in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004)*

---

**Q1158. The name of the Laccadive, Minicoy and Amindivi islands was changed to Lakshadweep by an Act of Parliament in**

- **A.** 1970
- **B.** 1971
- **C.** 1972
- **D.** 1973  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** The Laccadive, Minicoy and Amindivi Islands were renamed Lakshadweep through an Act of Parliament in 1973, which formally changed the territorial name to reflect its Sanskrit-derived designation. Why not the others: (A) 1970 predates the actual legislative action; (B) 1971 is when the union territory's first general elections were held, not when the name change occurred; (C) 1972 is one year before the correct year of the name-change act.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/)*

---

**Q1159. The Objectives Resolution was unanimously adopted by the Constituent Assembly on**

- **A.** 22nd January 1947  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** 26th November 1946
- **C.** 1st October 1948
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The Objectives Resolution, moved by Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru, was unanimously adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 22nd January 1947, making it one of the foundational documents that shaped the vision and principles of the Indian Constitution. This resolution outlined the sovereign, democratic, and secular character of the future Indian state. Why not the others: (B) 26th November 1946 was when the Constituent Assembly began deliberations on the draft Constitution, not when the Objectives Resolution was adopted; (C) 1st October 1948 falls well after the Constitution was drafted and was not significant for the Objectives Resolution; (D) is incorrect because option (A) is the verified correct date.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013)*

---

**Q1160. The minimum age required to become a member of Rajya Sabha is**

- **A.** 21 years
- **B.** 25 years
- **C.** 30 years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** 35 years

**Explanation:** The minimum age requirement to become a member of Rajya Sabha is 30 years as specified in Article 84 of the Indian Constitution. This higher age qualification reflects the upper house's intended role as a more experienced and deliberative chamber. Why not the others: (A) 21 years is the minimum age for Lok Sabha membership, not Rajya Sabha; (B) 25 years is not the constitutional minimum for either house; (D) 35 years is the minimum age requirement for becoming President or Vice President, not for Rajya Sabha membership.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011)*

---

**Q1161. The Objectives Resolution which laid down the main objectives to guide the deliberations of the Assembly was moved by**

- **A.** Sardar Patel
- **B.** Jawaharlal Nehru  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** K.M. Munshi
- **D.** B.R. Ambedkar

**Explanation:** Jawaharlal Nehru moved the Objectives Resolution on December 13, 1946, in the Constituent Assembly. This foundational resolution outlined the core principles and goals that would guide the drafting of the Indian Constitution, including sovereignty, democracy, equality, and justice. Why not the others: (A) Sardar Patel was the Deputy Chairman of the Constituent Assembly but did not move the Objectives Resolution; (C) K.M. Munshi was a member of the Drafting Committee but was not the one who moved this resolution; (D) B.R. Ambedkar was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee responsible for drafting the Constitution itself, not moving the Objectives Resolution.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005)*

---

**Q1162. The Parliament of India can make use of the residuary powers**

- **A.** at all times  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** only during national emergency
- **C.** during national emergency as well as constitutional emergency as well in a state
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** Article 248 of the Constitution grants Parliament residuary powers to make laws on any subject not enumerated in the State List or Concurrent List, and these powers can be exercised at all times without any emergency requirement. The residuary powers are a permanent constitutional feature that allows Parliament to legislate on unforeseen matters and are not contingent on any emergency situation. Why not the others: (B) incorrectly restricts residuary powers to only national emergencies when they apply continuously; (C) mistakenly adds conditions of constitutional emergency and state-specific situations, which are not prerequisites for residuary powers; (D) is incorrect because option (A) accurately reflects constitutional law.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002)*

---

**Q1163. The members of the state legislative assemblies are elected for a period of**

- **A.** 2 years
- **B.** 6 years
- **C.** 5 years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** 3 years

**Explanation:** Members of state legislative assemblies in India are elected for a term of 5 years, as stipulated in Article 172 of the Indian Constitution. The state legislature is dissolved after 5 years, and fresh elections are held unless it is dissolved earlier by the Governor under constitutional provisions. Why not the others: (A) 2 years is too short and not the constitutional term for state assemblies; (B) 6 years is the term for Rajya Sabha members, not state assembly members; (D) 3 years was the term under the Government of India Act 1919 but has not been the constitutional norm since independence.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015)*

---

**Q1164. The minimum age to qualify for election to the Lok Sabha is**

- **A.** 25 years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** 21 years
- **C.** 18 years
- **D.** 35 years

**Explanation:** The Constitution of India (Article 84) specifies that a person must be at least 25 years of age to be eligible for election to the Lok Sabha. This age requirement ensures that candidates have sufficient maturity and experience for legislative responsibilities. Why not the others: (B) 21 years is the minimum voting age in India, not the eligibility age for Lok Sabha candidature; (C) 18 years is the age of majority and voting eligibility, but below the candidature requirement; (D) 35 years is the minimum age requirement for the Rajya Sabha, not the Lok Sabha.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003)*

---

**Q1165. The preamble to our constitution includes all the following except**

- **A.** adult franchise  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** equality of status
- **C.** fraternity
- **D.** justice

**Explanation:** The Preamble to the Indian Constitution explicitly mentions the objectives of justice, liberty, equality, and fraternity but does not specifically include the term 'adult franchise' or universal adult suffrage. Adult franchise is guaranteed in Article 326 of the Constitution, not in the Preamble itself. Why not the others: (B) equality of status is directly mentioned in the Preamble's commitment to equality; (C) fraternity is explicitly stated as one of the core values in the Preamble; (D) justice is the very first word of the Preamble and encompasses social, economic, and political justice.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014)*

---

**Q1166. The Parliament can restrict or abrogate by law, fundamental rights with respect to**

- **A.** the members of the armed forces
- **B.** the forces charged with the maintenance of public order
- **C.** the persons employed in any bureau or other organization established by the state for purpose of intelligence
- **D.** All of the above  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** Article 33 of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to restrict or abrogate fundamental rights through law with respect to members of the armed forces, persons employed in intelligence agencies, and those in forces maintaining public order, in order to ensure their disciplinary efficiency and loyalty. Therefore, all three categories mentioned in options A, B, and C fall under Article 33's purview, making D the correct answer. Why not the others: (A) is only partially correct as it excludes other covered categories; (B) is only partially correct as it omits armed forces and intelligence personnel; (C) is only partially correct as it excludes armed forces and public order maintenance forces.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007)*

---

## Polity — Standard 8

**Q1167. The pension of a high court judge is charged to the**

- **A.** Consolidated Fund of India  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Consolidated Fund of the state where he last served
- **C.** Consolidated Funds of the different states where he has served
- **D.** Contingency Fund of India

**Explanation:** The pension of a high court judge is charged to the Consolidated Fund of India because judicial pensions for superior court judges are a central government obligation under the Constitution, ensuring uniform and independent provision of retirement benefits across the entire country. Why not the others: (B) the Consolidated Fund of a single state is incorrect because the judge's pension is not limited to any one state's finances; (C) splitting pensions across multiple state funds would be administratively impractical and constitutionally inappropriate; (D) the Contingency Fund is meant for temporary, unforeseen expenses, not recurring pension obligations.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003)*

---

**Q1168. The preamble declares India as a sovereign state which implies**

- **A.** India is free to conduct her internal affairs
- **B.** India is free to conduct her external affairs
- **C.** India is free to conduct here internal as well as external affairs  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** Sovereignty, as declared in the Preamble, means India possesses complete independence and self-determination in both its internal governance and external relations with other nations—neither is subject to external control or interference. This comprehensive freedom to manage domestic matters (internal affairs) and international relations (external affairs) is the core meaning of state sovereignty. Why not the others: (A) covers only internal affairs, leaving out the external dimension of sovereignty; (B) covers only external affairs, missing the internal autonomy that sovereignty guarantees; (D) is incorrect because options A and C together do constitute the full meaning of sovereignty.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013)*

---

**Q1169. The president can advance money to meet unforeseen expenses, pending authorization by Parliament, from**

- **A.** the Consolidated Fund of India
- **B.** the Contingency Fund  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** both the above funds
- **D.** None of the above

**Explanation:** The Contingency Fund of India is specifically established to allow the President to advance money for unforeseen expenses without prior parliamentary authorization, with Parliament's approval coming later. This fund exists precisely to handle emergencies that cannot wait for the regular budget process. Why not the others: (A) the Consolidated Fund requires parliamentary authorization before any withdrawal, not after; (C) while both funds exist, only the Contingency Fund permits advance without prior authorization; (D) option (B) is correct, so this is incorrect.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002005)*

---

**Q1170. The president can be impeached for**

- **A.** violating the constitution  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** disregarding Parliament
- **C.** for not taking the prime minister's advice
- **D.** All of the above

**Explanation:** The President can be impeached only for violating the Constitution, as specified in Article 61 of the Indian Constitution, which defines impeachment grounds as 'violation of the Constitution.' This is a specific legal standard, not a subjective or discretionary matter. Why not the others: (B) disregarding Parliament alone is not an impeachment ground—it must constitute a constitutional violation; (C) the President is not legally bound to follow the Prime Minister's advice in all matters, and refusal to do so is not grounds for impeachment; (D) since only constitutional violation qualifies, 'All of the above' is incorrect.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002011)*

---

**Q1171. The president demand for further reforms, attended with the dislocation caused by the non-cooperation movement, led the British government to appoint a Statutory Commission in 1927. This commission was headed by**

- **A.** Sri John Simon  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** Lord Chelmsford
- **C.** Lord Minto
- **D.** E.S. Montague

**Explanation:** The Statutory Commission appointed by the British government in 1927 to examine India's constitutional progress was headed by Sir John Simon, a British lawyer and politician. This commission, known as the Simon Commission, was sent to India in response to demands for further constitutional reforms and the disruptions caused by the non-cooperation movement. Why not the others: (B) Lord Chelmsford headed the earlier Montagu-Chelmsford Commission of 1918, not the 1927 commission; (C) Lord Minto was associated with the Minto-Morley Reforms of 1909, predating the 1927 appointment; (D) E.S. Montague was part of the 1918 Montagu-Chelmsford Commission, not the 1927 commission.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006)*

---

**Q1172. Mainstream Nationalism in India**

- **A.** was characterized by Chauvinism
- **B.** aimed at restoration of the Hindu state
- **C.** had national socialism as its ultimate goal
- **D.** aimed at emancipation from colonial rule  ✅ **(correct answer)**

**Explanation:** Mainstream nationalism in India, represented by the Indian National Congress and leaders like Gandhi and Nehru, was fundamentally driven by the goal of achieving independence from British colonial rule, rather than pursuing any particular domestic ideology. This anti-colonial struggle united diverse religious, regional, and ideological groups around the central objective of self-governance. Why not the others: (A) mainstream nationalism explicitly rejected chauvinism and communal divisions, emphasizing inclusive patriotism; (B) the primary aim was not religious restoration but secular independence for all Indians regardless of faith; (C) national socialism was not an articulated or pursued goal of the mainstream nationalist movement, which drew from various liberal, socialist, and Gandhian ideologies.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014)*

---

**Q1173. The office of the prime minister of India**

- **A.** rests on coronations
- **B.** is created by the Parliament
- **C.** is created by the constitution  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** All of the above

**Explanation:** The office of the Prime Minister is created by the Constitution of India (Part IX, Article 74), which establishes the executive structure and defines the PM's role as the head of government. This constitutional creation is the legal and institutional foundation of the office. Why not the others: (A) rests on coronations is incorrect because the PM's office is not based on ceremonial coronation but on constitutional provisions; (B) is created by the Parliament is incorrect because while Parliament elects the PM, the office itself is constitutionally established, not created by parliamentary action alone; (D) All of the above is incorrect because options A and B are factually wrong, making this option untenable.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002015)*

---

**Q1174. The president addresses both the Houses of Parliament assembled together**

- **A.** during emergency session summoned for the purpose
- **B.** every session
- **C.** first session after each general election and the first session of each year  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** any session

**Explanation:** The President addresses both Houses of Parliament assembled together on specific ceremonial occasions: at the commencement of the first session after each general election and at the beginning of the first session of each year, as established by constitutional practice and Article 87 of the Indian Constitution. Why not the others: (A) emergency sessions are summoned for specific urgent matters and do not follow the presidential address protocol; (B) the President does not address both Houses during every session, only at designated ceremonial occasions; (D) the presidential address is not given during any arbitrary session but only at the prescribed constitutional moments.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004)*

---

**Q1175. The members of the standing committee are taken from the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha in the ratio of**

- **A.** two and one respectively  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** three and one respectively
- **C.** four and one respectively
- **D.** equal members for both the Houses

**Explanation:** The standing committee members are drawn from the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha in a 2:1 ratio, meaning for every two members from Lok Sabha, one member comes from Rajya Sabha. This composition is designed to give greater representation to the Lok Sabha, which is the directly elected and larger house of Parliament. Why not the others: (B) a 3:1 ratio is not the standard proportion used for standing committees; (C) a 4:1 ratio would give disproportionate weightage to Lok Sabha beyond the actual practice; (D) equal representation from both houses contradicts the established 2:1 formula that prioritizes Lok Sabha.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002012)*

---

**Q1176. The members of the Rajya Sabha are elected by**

- **A.** the people
- **B.** Lok Sabha
- **C.** elected members of the legislative assembly  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** elected members of the legislative council

**Explanation:** Members of Rajya Sabha are elected by the elected members of the Assemblies of States and Union territories in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote. Ref: http://rajyasabha.nic.in/rsnew/faq/freaq1.asp

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/)*

---

**Q1177. Preamble enshrines the ideals of liberty, equality and fraternity - ideals inspired by the**

- **A.** Russian Revolution
- **B.** Irish Revolution
- **C.** French Revolution  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **D.** US Constitution

**Explanation:** The preamble of the Indian Constitution explicitly incorporates the ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity, which are the foundational principles of the French Revolution (1789). These three concepts became the rallying cry of the French Revolution and were later embedded in the Indian Constitution by Dr. Ambedkar and the drafting committee. Why not the others: (A) The Russian Revolution (1917) focused on communism and workers' rights, not these three specific ideals; (B) The Irish Revolution was primarily a nationalist and independence movement, not ideologically centered on these universal principles; (D) While the US Constitution influenced Indian constitutional thought, the specific trinity of liberty, equality, and fraternity originates from French revolutionary thought.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002007)*

---

**Q1178. The members of Lok Sabha hold office for a term of**

- **A.** 4 years
- **B.** 5 years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** 6 years
- **D.** 3 years

**Explanation:** The members of Lok Sabha hold office for a term of 5 years, as specified in Article 172 of the Indian Constitution. This five-year term applies to the lower house of Parliament and is counted from the date the house is constituted, unless dissolved earlier. Why not the others: (A) 4 years is the term for state legislative assemblies, not Lok Sabha; (C) 6 years is the term for Rajya Sabha members (though they serve staggered six-year terms with one-third retiring every two years); (D) 3 years was historically relevant to earlier legislative bodies but is not the current constitutional provision for Lok Sabha.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002002)*

---

## Polity — Standard 9

**Q1179. The powers to legislate with respect to any matter not enumerated in any of the three lists are mentioned as residuary powers. Which of the following is empowered to determine finally as to whether or not a particular matter falls in this category**

- **A.** Lok Sabha
- **B.** Judiciary  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** Rajya Sabha
- **D.** Parliament

**Explanation:** The Judiciary, particularly the Supreme Court, has the power to interpret the Constitution and determine whether a matter falls under residuary powers (List I, residuary entry in the Seventh Schedule). This judicial review function allows courts to settle disputes about the constitutional division of legislative powers. Why not the others: (A) Lok Sabha cannot independently make final determinations on constitutional matters as it is part of the legislature being regulated; (C) Rajya Sabha, despite representing states, does not have final interpretive authority over constitutional divisions of power; (D) Parliament as a whole cannot be the final arbiter of its own constitutional limits—an independent judiciary must serve that role.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002006)*

---

**Q1180. The minimum age required to become the prime minister of India is**

- **A.** 25 years  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** 30 years
- **C.** 40 years
- **D.** 35 years

**Explanation:** The Constitution of India (Article 79) stipulates that a person must be at least 25 years old to be a member of Parliament (Lok Sabha), and since the Prime Minister must be a member of Parliament, the minimum age requirement for the office of Prime Minister is 25 years. Why not the others: (B) 30 years is a higher threshold not stipulated in the Constitution for PM eligibility; (C) 40 years is even higher and not the constitutional requirement; (D) 35 years is also higher than the actual constitutional minimum age.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002014)*

---

**Q1181. The preamble says that the state in India will assure the dignity of the individual. The constitution seeks to achieve this object by guaranteeing**

- **A.** equal fundamental rights to each citizen  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** the right to adequate means of livelihood to each individual
- **C.** just and humane conditions of work to each individual
- **D.** equal wages for equal work to each individual irrespective of sex

**Explanation:** The Preamble of the Indian Constitution explicitly mentions 'dignity of the individual' as a foundational objective, and Part III of the Constitution achieves this through the guaranteed Fundamental Rights which apply equally to all citizens, thereby protecting individual dignity across all spheres. Why not the others: (B) the right to adequate means of livelihood is a Directive Principle, not a Fundamental Right; (C) just and humane conditions of work is also a Directive Principle under Article 43, not a primary constitutional guarantee for dignity; (D) equal wages for equal work regardless of sex is a specific Directive Principle in Article 39(d), not the broad constitutional mechanism for assuring dignity.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002003)*

---

**Q1182. The parliament works through committees which**

- **A.** help the government in the formulation of policy
- **B.** exercise effective control over government on a regular and continuing basis  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **C.** ensure that the parliament strictly adheres to the provision of the constitution and the prescribed parliamentary procedure
- **D.** both (a) and (b)

**Explanation:** Parliamentary committees, particularly standing committees, serve as essential mechanisms for legislative oversight and control. They scrutinize government actions, examine budgets, review bills, and conduct inquiries on a regular and continuous basis, thereby exercising effective democratic control over the executive. Why not the others: (A) while committees may provide input, their primary constitutional function is oversight and control rather than policy formulation, which is a government responsibility; (C) though committees respect parliamentary procedures, their main role is not procedural compliance but substantive government scrutiny; (D) is incorrect because option (A) mischaracterizes the committees' primary function, and combining it with (B) would be inaccurate.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002013)*

---

**Q1183. The president can dissolve the Lok Sabha on**

- **A.** advice of the prime minister  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** advice of the chief justice of India
- **C.** recommendation of Lok Sabha
- **D.** recommendation of the Rajya Sabha

**Explanation:** Under Article 85(2) of the Indian Constitution, the President has the power to dissolve the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister, as the leader of the government, holds the constitutional authority to recommend dissolution, and the President acts on this advice. Why not the others: (B) the Chief Justice of India has no role in dissolution matters as it is a purely executive function; (C) the Lok Sabha cannot recommend its own dissolution—dissolution is a presidential power exercised on executive advice; (D) the Rajya Sabha, being the upper house, has no authority to recommend dissolution of the lower house.

*Source: [https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004](https://www.indiabix.com/general-knowledge/indian-politics/002004)*

---

**Q1184. The position of a chief minister is**

- **A.** similar to that of the prime minister  ✅ **(correct answer)**
- **B.** identical to that of the president
